title: SDN Configuration Change and Rollback Audit
dataSources: Configuration change events, user, object, before/after
spl:
index=sdn sourcetype="sdn:audit"
| search (action="modified" OR action="deleted" OR action="rollback")
| table _time, user, object_type, object_name, action, change_summary
| sort -_time
description: Unauthorized fabric or policy changes can cause outages or security gaps. Auditing changes and rollbacks supports change control and incident analysis.
value: Unauthorized fabric or policy changes can cause outages or security gaps. Auditing changes and rollbacks supports change control and incident analysis.
implementation: Ingest controller and fabric audit logs. Alert on change to critical objects (e.g., tenant, contract, segment) without change ticket. Report on change frequency and rollback rate. Integrate with change management.
visualization: Table (recent changes), Timeline (change events), Bar chart (changes by user).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: APIC/NSX/controller audit logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Configuration change events, user, object, before/after.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Ingest controller and fabric audit logs. Alert on change to critical objects (e.g., tenant, contract, segment) without change ticket. Report on change frequency and rollback rate. Integrate with change management.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=sdn sourcetype="sdn:audit"
| search (action="modified" OR action="deleted" OR action="rollback")
| table _time, user, object_type, object_name, action, change_summary
| sort -_time
```

Understanding this SPL

**SDN Configuration Change and Rollback Audit** — Unauthorized fabric or policy changes can cause outages or security gaps. Auditing changes and rollbacks supports change control and incident analysis.

Documented **Data sources**: Configuration change events, user, object, before/after. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): APIC/NSX/controller audit logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: sdn; **sourcetype**: sdn:audit. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=sdn, sourcetype="sdn:audit". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• Pipeline stage (see **SDN Configuration Change and Rollback Audit**): table _time, user, object_type, object_name, action, change_summary
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**SDN Configuration Change and Rollback Audit** — Unauthorized fabric or policy changes can cause outages or security gaps.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Change.All_Changes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.



Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the controller audit or change log (NSX, APIC, or SDN) and change tickets for the same objects.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (recent changes), Timeline (change events), Bar chart (changes by user).
grandmaExplanation: Unauthorized fabric or policy changes can cause outages or security gaps — so traffic problems, routing mistakes, and risky changes surface fast.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object, All_Changes.user | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Planned change windows, automation maintenance, and rollback drills that touch the same objects.
